segments.s353-g49: همه روم is a noun phrase where همه ('all/everyone') precedes روم (Rum/the Romans) and functions as the subject: 'all of Rum' or 'all the Romans.' با هدیه و با نثار are two prepositional phrases (با + noun) placed before the verb to indicate accompaniment/what they brought; the repetition با ... و با ... is emphatic. نثار here is a nominal use meaning 'offerings/tribute.'
segments.s353-g50: برفتند is the 3rd-person plural past of رفتن with the literary prefix بر- (an archaic/classical form) meaning 'they went/returned.' شادان is an adjectival/advantageous predicate used adverbially after the verb to indicate manner ('joyfully'). بر نامدار is a prepositional phrase with بر meaning 'toward/to' and نامدار 'the renowned one,' expressing destination.
segments.s353-g41: Word order is slightly archaic/poetic: the preposition بر + object قیصر precedes the verb آمد and the subject سپه appears after آمد. بر قیصر آمد here means 'came upon/attacked Caesar' and تاخته is a past participial/adjectival form meaning 'having rushed/charged'.
segments.s353-g42: به پیروزی is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in/with victory' describing the state; و simply connects the next phrase; گردن افراخته is a nominal phrase 'neck(s) uplifted' used idiomatically for 'proud / with heads held high' (افراخته is participial/adjectival).
segments.s353-g43: ز لشکر ('from the army' or 'out of the ranks') gives the vantage; چو is the conjunction 'when'; بدیدش is the past form بدید (classical b- prefix) plus the enclitic -ش 'him', so 'he saw him'; به راه 'on the road/way' is an adverbial phrase.
segments.s353-g44: ز شادی here indicates the motivating circumstance 'out of/with joy' or 'because of joy'; پذیره (reception) together with شدش expresses that he received/welcomed him — شدش is the classical past auxiliary-like 'became/was' with the enclitic -ش referring to the person received; با سپاه adds 'with (his) army'. The whole phrase means 'he received/welcomed him joyfully with his troops.'
segments.s353-g45: سر و چشم 'head and eyes' names the parts kissed; آن نامور 'that famed one' is the object phrase; بوس is used as the object 'a kiss' or verbal noun and داد is the verb 'gave', a conventional Persian idiom meaning 'gave a kiss' i.e. 'kissed'.
segments.s353-g46: جهان‌آفرین را is the definite object marked with را ('the Creator'); همی is an archaic/poetic particle indicating continuous or habitual aspect ('still/continually'); کرد یاد literally 'made remembrance' = 'remembered' or 'kept remembering.'
segments.s353-g47: وزان جایگه 'from that place' is a compact prepositional phrase (وزان = و + ز آن 'and from that'); بازگشتند is the past plural 'they returned'; شاد is a predicate adjective 'joyful' modifying the subject (they).
segments.s353-g48: سپهبد is the agent 'the spahbed' (a high military rank); کلاه کیان is a genitival phrase 'the hat/crown of the Kayanids' (i.e. royal crown); برنهاد is a classical past verb meaning 'placed upon' or 'set on'. The clause describes the spahbed placing the royal crown.
segments.s353-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction here (=“when”) introducing the clause. خورشید is the subject; شد is the past tense of شدن (“became/rose”) placed after the subject as is normal in Persian narrative. بر سر کوه زرد is a prepositional phrase (“on the summit of the yellow mountain”) where بر سر = “on the top of” and زرد is the adjective following the noun کوه (typical noun–adjective order).
segments.s353-g2: نماند is the 3rd-person past negative of ماندن (“did not remain/was not left”) and governs the following noun phrase. آن زمان روزگار نبرد is a determiner + noun + noun compound: آن زمان = “that time/then,” روزگار = “time/period,” and نبرد = “battle”; together they mean “that time of battle” or “time for fighting.” The verb precedes that noun phrase in this literary inversion.
segments.s353-g3: شب آمد is a simple past clause (“night came/fell”), with subject then verb. یکی here functions as an indefinite/article-like modifier (“a certain / a single”) modifying پرده آبنوس; پرده = “veil/curtain” and آبنوس describes it as ebony/very dark. The phrase is compact and poetic (one veil of ebony).
segments.s353-g4: بپوشید is the past tense (3rd person) of پوشیدن used transitively here in the sense “covered/was thrown over.” بر چهره is the prepositional phrase “over/on the face,” where چهره = “face/appearance.” سندروس is the object of that preposition (a poetic name for sandalwood/fragrant wood or a personified perfume), so the whole means “the veil covered Sandros’s face.”
segments.s353-g5: چو again = “when.” خورشید is the subject; آگاه شد is a compound verb meaning “became aware” or “learned.” ازان کوشش is a prepositional/object phrase with ازان = “of/from that” and کوشش = “attempt/effort,” so the clause reads “when the sun became aware of that effort.” Word order places the verb at the end in the usual literary pattern.
segments.s353-g6: ز = “from.” برج کمان is a compound (literally “tower/constellation of the bow” — poetically the bow-sign or an archer’s tower). بر سر گاه شد is idiomatic: بر سر گاه شدن/شدن = “to reach/take one’s place at the appointed spot/time” or “to come to the appointed hour.” شد is the past-tense marker showing the change/state. The whole segment describes movement or arrival from the bow-sign/tower to the appointed time/place.
segments.s353-g7: ببد is an archaic/poetic past form equivalent to شد/بود (“became/was”) and here governs the following noun phrase. چشمه روز = “the fountain/source of the day” (a poetic image for dawn). چون = “like/as,” and سندروس is the simile target (sandalwood/fragrant wood), so the clause means “the dawn became like sandalwood.”
segments.s353-g8: ز هر سو = “from every side” (ز = “from,” هر = “every,” سو = “side”). برآمد is the past tense “arose/issued forth.” دم نای و کوس = “the breath/sound of the ney (reed flute) and the drum,” where دم denotes the emitted breath/sound of the instruments. The phrase describes music/signal sounds emerging from all directions.
segments.s353-g17: بجنبید is the past third‑person singular of جنبیدن ('to move/stir') and occurs before the subject (inverted word order common in epic Persian) so it reads 'moved (he)'. گشتاسپ is the subject (proper name). از پیش صف is a prepositional phrase: از 'from' + پیش 'front/before' + صف 'line/formation', giving 'from the front of the line.'
segments.s353-g18: یکی باره (literally 'one instance') is an adverbial compound meaning 'suddenly/at once'. زیر اژدهایی به کف describes location/possession: زیر 'under' or 'beneath' + اژدهایی 'a dragon' (indefinite) + به کف 'into the palm/hand', so the phrase conveys that, suddenly, a dragon ended up in (or under) his hand — the clause is somewhat elliptic (classical Persian often omits an explict verb like 'grasped'), but functions as a single sudden action tied to the preceding verb.
segments.s353-g19: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' and modifies the following speech verb. گفت here functions as the past 'said' (the lexeme list gives conversational senses but it functions as the reporting verb). الیاس is the speaker's name; با انجمن ('with the assembly') indicates Elias spoke in the assembly / addressed the gathering (با used as 'in the presence of' or 'to').
segments.s353-g20: که introduces a content clause ('that'). قیصر is 'the Caesar/emperor' (subject of the subordinate clause). همی is the archaic particle marking ongoing/future action or emphasis (here it helps form the future sense). باژ is a noun 'cry/noise' (object). خواهد is the auxiliary/verb meaning 'will/wishes' (future). ز is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'because of/from' and links باژ to من ('me') — 'will raise a cry about/against me.'
segments.s353-g21: چو here functions as a temporal/conditional 'when/if'. بر در = 'at/on the gate' (بر + در where در is 'gate' here). چنین 'such' modifies اژدها 'dragon'. باشدش is consists of باشد (subjunctive/optative or present of 'to be') with the clitic ش = 'for him', so 'it be/exists for him' — together: 'If/when such a dragon is at the gate for him.'
segments.s353-g22: ازیرا is an unclear/possibly proper form in the text (here treated as a name or fixed phrase). منش means 'origin/lineage' (or 'character' in other contexts) and links to what follows. بابها is the plural of باب 'gate/door' (here 'gates/doors'). باشدش again is 'will be/to him' (باشد + the clitic ش 'for him'), giving a result clause: 'then from Azyra his origin the gates will be his' — literal wording reflects classical, compressed syntax.
segments.s353-g23: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. Word order is typical: temporal marker + subject (گشتاسپ) + object marked by را (الیاس را) + verb دید ('saw'). The final گفت is the reporting verb 'he said' that introduces the following content clause.
segments.s353-g24: که introduces the reported content ('that'). اکنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. هنرها is the plural of هنر ('arts/skills/virtues') as the subject of the clause. نباید is the impersonal/modal 'must not/should not'. نهفت is the verb/adjective 'be hidden/concealed' — together: 'that now the skills must not be hidden.'
segments.s353-g33: ز پیش سواران is a prepositional phrase 'from in front of the horsemen'. کشانش is the present-participle or verbal adjective کشان ('dragging') with the enclitic object -ش ('him'). ببرد is a past narrative verb 'took/removed' (classical b‑ prefix on the root برد); together the clause says that he dragged him away from the horsemen.
segments.s353-g34: بیاورد is a past narrative verb 'he brought (him)'. و is the coordinating 'and'. نزدیک قیصر functions adverbially as 'near/at the presence of Caesar'. سپرد is the past verb 'handed over/entrusted'. The object 'him' carried earlier in -ش remains understood through the coordinated verbs.
segments.s353-g35: بیاورد is the past narrative 'he brought'. لشکر is the direct object 'the army/host'. به پیش سپاه is a locative/goal phrase 'up before the (other) host' (به + پیش = 'towards/in front of'). The clause means he caused the army to appear in front of the troops.
segments.s353-g36: به کردار باد is an adverbial simile: به + کردار = 'in the manner/character of', باد = 'the wind' → 'like the wind'. اندر آمد is the past 'entered/came in'. ز راه is an ablative 'from the road'. Altogether: 'it came from the road like the wind' (i.e. swiftly).
segments.s353-g37: از ایشان 'from them' marks the source. چه مایه is an interrogative/indefinite phrase here meaning 'what amount/what plunder' (مایه = resource/booty). گرفت is the past 'he seized/took'. و بکشت is 'and killed' — بکشت is an archaic/perfective form with the b‑ prefix used in epic narrative for a past action.
segments.s353-g38: بکشتند is the past third-person plural 'they killed'. مر is an older enclitic (here functioning like a dative/ethical 'to me' or an emphatic particle tied to the narrator) and is common in epic diction. هرک آمد به مشت is a relative/restrictive phrase 'whoever came into (their) fist/reach' (آمد = 'came'; به مشت = 'into the fist' idiomatically 'within reach'). The clause means they slaughtered anyone who came near.
segments.s353-g39: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. رومی is the subject 'the Romans/Byzantines'. پس‌اندر is an adverb 'afterward/thereafter'. هم‌آواز (lit. 'same-voice') is an adjective/adverb meaning 'of one mind' or 'in unison'. شد is the past 'became' — 'when the Romans afterward fell into agreement.'
segments.s353-g40: چو again = 'when'. گشتاسپ is the proper name 'Gushtasp'. زان جایگه is an ablative phrase 'from that place' (زان = 'from that'). باز شد is a classical expression meaning 'he withdrew/returned/left' — باز here conveys the idea of 'back/away' combined with شد 'became/was' to mean 'he withdrew from that place.'
segments.s353-g9: چکاچاک is an onomatopoeic noun/adverb meaning a metallic clatter or clinking; it functions as the phenomenon that ‘arose’. برخاست is a simple past intransitive verb 'arose/rose up'. از هر دو روی is a prepositional phrase giving the source/direction ('from both sides'), with هر دو modifying روی ('both sides'). Word order is poetic (sound first, then verb, then source).
segments.s353-g10: ز خون شد is a compact past construction: شد (past of 'become') takes ز خون ('from/of blood') to mean 'became filled/turned into blood'. همه رزمگه is the patient/theme 'the whole battlefield' (همه = entire). جوی جوی is a repeated noun for emphasis meaning 'in streams' or 'stream upon stream' (poetic reduplication).
segments.s353-g11: بیامد is past 'came'. سبک here modifies قیصر (understood as a force belonging to the Caesar) and is best read as 'light' (light‑armed or swift) in this martial context. از میمنه is a directional/ablative phrase 'from the right (side/flank)' where میمنه means the right side (also carries auspicious connotation in classical usage). Word order puts the verb before the noun phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s353-g12: دو داماد را is the definite/accusative object ('the two sons‑in‑law', marked by را). کرد is the lexical past 'he made/did/placed' used here to mean 'set/placed'. پیش is an adverb/preposition 'in front' (indicating position). بنه in this line is an archaic/poetic verbal or nominal use meaning 'to set/place' or 'a position' — this is an irregular/old usage (not the literal 'pistachio' or 'heap' in the dictionary list) and stands as the element expressing the placement. Overall: 'he placed the two sons‑in‑law forward.'
segments.s353-g13: ابر is used figuratively as 'a cloud' of troops or a swelling mass (a common epic image). میمنه follows it to specify 'the right (side/flank)' that this cloud occupies. پور قیصر is a patronymic/appositive 'son of the Caesar', and سقیل is a proper name in apposition to that phrase. The whole sequence strings appositions and epithets in typical epic compression.
segments.s353-g14: ابر میسره parallels the previous phrase: ابر ('cloud') qualified by میسره (here 'the left flank' — an epithet identifying the side). قیصر names the owner/commander (the emperor). و connects to کوس و پیل ('drums and elephants') — two coordinating martial items. Note میسره as 'left' is a classical usage; the following nouns list the instruments and beasts accompanying that flank.
segments.s353-g15: دهاده is a collective/quantifying adjective meaning 'many' or 'tenfold' (a multitudinous host). برآمد is past 'arose/issued forth'. ز هر دو سپاه is the ablative phrase 'from both armies/hosts'. The noun phrase precedes the verb in this poetic line but the syntactic relation is subject → verb → source phrase.
segments.s353-g16: تو گفتی is the second‑person past 'you said' (addressing someone); here گفتی functions as the speech verb introducing the reported phrase. برآویخت is an intransitive past 'rose up' or 'leapt up'. با is the preposition 'with' or 'together with' and introduces the companion/epithet شید ماه: شید modifies ماه as an epithet ('shîd‑month/moon'), where شید here is best read as 'beloved/moon‑like' and ماه is figurative 'the moon' = 'the beautiful one' (i.e., 'the moon-faced beloved'). Together they yield 'you said, “the moon‑faced one rose up.”'
segments.s353-g25: برانگیختند is a past, 3rd-person plural verb ('they sprang up/roused'); in Classical Persian the verb can appear before the subject. اسپ (steed) is the direct noun being roused; هر دو سوار is the subject phrase 'both riders' (هر دو = 'both, each of the two', سوار = 'rider/horseman'). The word order here is verb + object + subject, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s353-g26: This short nominal phrase lists the riders' equipment. نیزه و تیر = 'spear and arrows' joined by و ('and'). جوشن گذار functions as a compound-like description meaning 'clad in/ wearing a cuirass' (جوشن = armor). ابا here is archaic/uncertain in this context (it does not fit the usual sense 'to refuse'); likely an old or dialectal verbal element indicating bearing/possession or a scribal variant — the exact nuance is uncertain.
segments.s353-g27: ازان is an adverbial 'thereupon/from that'. لشکر الیاس is a genitival noun phrase 'the army/host of Elias' (noun + proper name). بگشاد is an older/perfective form indicating an opening or making wide (here 'made an opening'); the final شست in the manuscript is irregular in meaning here and does not match its ordinary senses, so its precise function is likely idiomatic or orthographic.
segments.s353-g28: که introduces a result/purpose clause ('so that' / 'that'). گشتاسپ را uses the object marker را marking Gushtasp as the definite object. برکند is a present/subjunctive-stem verb meaning 'uproot / dislodge / pull away' used here as the action hoped-for/caused. کار پست is a compound noun phrase 'an ignoble/base deed' (کار = 'deed/affair', پست = 'base/despicable'), the complement of برکند.
segments.s353-g29: بزد is a past tense 3rd-person singular 'he struck / smote'. The following nominal phrase gives the instrument and agent: نیزه (spear) and گشتاسپ (Gushtasp) — classical word order allows the verb before agent. بر + جوشنش = 'against his cuirass' (بر = 'on/against', جوشنش = 'his armor'; the pronominal suffix ش = 'his' is attached).
segments.s353-g30: بخست here literally 'closed/fastened' is used idiomatically of the spear becoming fixed/stuck; آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that moment' (آن = 'that/then', زمان = 'moment/time'). کارزاری qualifying تنش = 'his warlike/martial body' (کارزاری = 'martial', تن = 'body', ش = 'his'), i.e. his battle-gear or war‑shirt; the clause as a whole says the spear fixed in/onto his war-gear at that instant.
segments.s353-g31: بیفگندش is a compound past-form verb 'they threw/cast him' (ش = 'him' as enclitic object). از + باره = 'from the burden/saddle/load' (باره here = 'load/burden' i.e. pack or saddle). برسان is a past or narrative infinitive-like form 'brought' or 'brought him to (somewhere)'; مست is a predicate adjective 'drunk' describing the state in which they brought him. The sequence narrates successive actions by the captors.
segments.s353-g32: بیازید is an older/poetic perfective meaning 'they opened/unloosed' or 'they made open'; و = 'and' links actions. بگرفت is past 'they seized / took hold of'. دستش = 'his hand' (دست + ش enclitic = his). به دست = an idiom 'in (their) hand(s)' (به = 'into/in', دست = 'hand'), so the clause means 'they unbound/loosed him and took/seized his hand in theirs.'

sentences.s353-s25: All of Rum, with gifts and offerings, went joyfully to the renowned one.
sentences.s353-s21: The army rushed upon Caesar, victorious and with necks held high.
sentences.s353-s22: When Caesar saw him from the ranks on the road, he welcomed him with joy and with his troops.
sentences.s353-s23: He kissed the head and eyes of that famed one, and kept remembering the Creator.
sentences.s353-s24: From that place they returned, joyful; the spahbed set the Kayanian crown (hat) upon him.
sentences.s353-s1: When the sun rose upon the yellow mountain's summit, there remained no more time for battle.
sentences.s353-s2: Night came; a single ebony veil covered the face of Sandros.
sentences.s353-s3: When the sun learned of that effort, it left the sign (tower) of the bow and reached the appointed hour.
sentences.s353-s4: The fountain of day became like sandalwood; from every side the breath of flute and drum rose.
sentences.s353-s9: Gushtasp moved from the front of the line; suddenly, in one instant, a dragon was in his palm.
sentences.s353-s10: Thus Elias said to the assembly that the Caesar will raise a cry against me.
sentences.s353-s11: If such a dragon is at the gate for him, then from Azyra his origin the gates will be his.
sentences.s353-s12: When Gushtasp saw Elias, he said that now the skills should not be hidden.
sentences.s353-s17: He dragged him from before the horsemen, brought him, and handed him over close to Caesar.
sentences.s353-s18: He brought the army up before the host; it came from the road like the wind.
sentences.s353-s19: From them he took whatever plunder there was and killed; they slew whoever came within reach of their fists.
sentences.s353-s20: When the Romans afterwards became of one accord, Gushtasp withdrew from that place.
sentences.s353-s5: A clanging rose from both sides; the entire battlefield turned into streams of blood.
sentences.s353-s6: The emperor's light troops came from the right; he placed two sons‑in‑law in front.
sentences.s353-s7: On the right, the emperor's son Saqīl like a cloud; on the left, the emperor's cloud — and drums and elephants.
sentences.s353-s8: Many (a host) sprang up from both armies; you said, 'the moon‑faced one rose up.'
sentences.s353-s13: Both riders sprang onto their steeds, bearing spear and arrows and clad in mail.
sentences.s353-s14: Thereupon Elias's army made an opening, that the base deed might wrench Gushtasp away.
sentences.s353-s15: Gushtasp struck with his spear against his cuirass; at that moment the spear stuck fast in his war-gear.
sentences.s353-s16: They flung him from his saddle and brought him in drunk; they unbound him and seized his hand in theirs.
